elements.sent-2411-001: Serov \institute{ Kazan Federal University, Kazan, Russia.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Let us consider the Multiple String Matching Problem.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this problem, we consider a long string, denoted by $t$, of length $n$.
elements.sent-2411-004: This string is referred to as a text.
elements.sent-2411-005: We also consider a sequence of $m$ strings, denoted by $S$, which we refer to as a dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-006: The total length of all strings from the dictionary is represented by the variable L.
elements.sent-2411-007: The objective is to identify all instances of strings from the dictionary within the text.
elements.sent-2411-008: The standard classical solution to this problem is Aho–Corasick Algorithm that has $O(n+L)$ query and time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-009: At the same time, the classical lower bound for the problem is the same $\Omega(n+L)$.
elements.sent-2411-010: We propose a quantum algorithm with $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n +m\log n)$ query complexity and $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n}\log b+m\log n)=O^*(n+\sqrt{mL )$ time complexity, where $b$ is the maximal length of strings from the dictionary.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: This improvement is particularly significant in the case of dictionaries comprising long words.
elements.sent-2411-012: Our algorithm's complexity is equal to the quantum lower bound $O(n + \sqrt{mL )$, up to a log factor.}
elements.sent-2411-013: In some sense, our algorithm can be viewed as a quantum analogue of the Aho–Corasick algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-014: In this problem, we have a long string $t$ of a length $n$ that we call a text; and a sequence of $m$ strings $S$ that we call a dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-015: The total length of all strings from the dictionary is $L$, and the maximal length of the dictionary's strings is $b$.
elements.sent-2411-016: Our goal is to find all positions of strings from the dictionary in the text.
elements.sent-2411-017: The standard classical solution to this problem is Aho–Corasick Algorithm \cite{ac75 that has $O(n+L)$ time and query complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-018: We are interested in a quantum algorithm for this problem.
elements.sent-2411-019: There are many examples of quantum algorithms \cite{nc2010,a2017,k2022lecturenotes,aazksw2019part1 that are faster than classical counterparts \cite{dw2001,quantumzoo .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Particularly, we can find such examples among problems for strings processing \cite{aaksv2022,aj2021,kkmsy2022,ki2019,kbcw2024,ke2022,kiv2022,kk2021,kb2022,kr2021b,kr2021a,kszm2022,l2020,l2020conf,m2017,ks2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: One such problem is the String matching problem.
elements.sent-2411-022: That is checking whether a string $s$ is a substring of a string $t$.
elements.sent-2411-023: It is very similar to the Multiple String Matching Problem but with only one string in the dictionary.
elements.sent-2411-024: Additionally, in the String matching problem, we should find any position of $s$ not all of them.
elements.sent-2411-025: The last difference can be significant for quantum algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-026: For this problem, we can use, for example, Knuth–Morris–Pratt algorithm \cite{cormen2001,kmp77 in the classical case with $O(n+k)$ time and query complexity, where $n$ is the length of $t$ and $k$ is the length of $s$.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Other possible algorithms can be Backward Oracle Matching \cite{acr1999 , Wu-Manber \cite{wm1994 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: In the quantum case, we can use Ramesh-Vinay algorithm \cite{rv2003 with $O\left(\sqrt{n}\log{\sqrt{\frac{n {k }\log n + \sqrt{k (\log k)^2\right)=$ query complexity.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: We can say that query and time complexity of the Ramesh-Vinay algorithm is $O^*(\sqrt{n +\sqrt{k )$, where $O^*$ notation hides log factors.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: If we naively apply these algorithms to the Multiple String Matching Problem, then we obtain $O(mn+L)$ complexity in the classical case, and $O\left(mn\log{\sqrt{\frac{n {b }\log n + m^{1.5}\sqrt{L (\log b)^2\right)=O^*(mn+m^{1.5}\sqrt{L )$ in the quantum case.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the classical case, Aho–Corasick Algorithm \cite{ac75 has better complexity that is $O(n+L)$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Another possible algorithm that can work better in average is the Commentz-Walter algorithm \cite{cw1979 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: In the quantum case, we suggest a new algorithm for the Multiple String Matching Problem with $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n +m\log n)$ query complexity and $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n}\log b+m\log n)$ time complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-034: So, we can say that both are $O^*(n+\sqrt{mL )$.}
elements.sent-2411-035: At the same time, we show that the lower bound for classical complexity is $\Omega(n+l)$, and for quantum complexity, it is $\Omega(n+\sqrt{mL )$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: It means, that the Aho–Corasick Algorithm reaches the lower bound, and our algorithm reaches the lower bound up to a log factor.
elements.sent-2411-037: At the same time, if $O(m)$ strings of the dictionary have at least $\omega(\log n)$ length, then our quantum algorithm shows speed-up.
elements.sent-2411-038: The structure of this paper is as follows: Section \ref{sec:prelims contains preliminaries, used data structures and algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Section \ref{sec:aho–corasick provides a quantum algorithm for the Multiple String Matching Problem.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The final Section \ref{sec:concl concludes the paper and contains open questions.}
elements.sent-2411-041: For a string $u=(u_1,\dots,u_M)$, let $|u|=M$ be a length of the string.
elements.sent-2411-042: Let $u[i:j]=(u_i,\dots,u_j)$ be a substring of $u$ from $i$-th to $j$-th symbol.
elements.sent-2411-044: In the paper, we say that a string $u$ is less than a string $v$ if $u$ precedes $v$ in the lexicographical order.
elements.sent-2411-045: Suppose, we have a string $t$ and a sequence of strings $S=(s^1,\dots,s^m)$.
elements.sent-2411-046: We call the set $S$ a dictionary, and the the string $t$ a text.
elements.sent-2411-047: Let $L=|s^1|+\dots+|s^m|$ be the total length of the dictionary, and $b=\max\{|s^1|,\dots,|s^m|\}$ be the maximal length of dictionary's strings.
elements.sent-2411-048: Let an integer $n=|t|$ be the length of $t$.
elements.sent-2411-049: The problem is to find positions in $t$ for all strings from $S$.
elements.sent-2411-050: Formally, we want to find a sequence of indexes ${\cal I =(I_1,\dots,I_m)$, where $I_j=(i_{j,1 ,\dots,i_{j,k_j )$ such that $t[i_{j,x :i_{j,x +|s^j|-1]=s^j$ for each $x\in\{1,\dots,k_j\}, j\in\{1,\dots,m\}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: Here $k_j$ is the number of occurrences of $s^j$ in the string $t$.
elements.sent-2411-052: A suffix array \cite{mm90 is an array $suf=(suf_1,\dots,suf_{l )$ for a string $u$ where $l=|u|$ is the length of the string.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The suffix array is the lexicographical order for all suffixes of $u$.
elements.sent-2411-055: Let $ConstructSuffixArray(u)$ be a procedure that constructs the suffix array for the string $u$.
elements.sent-2411-056: The query and time complexity of the procedure are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-057: Let longest common prefix (LCP) of two strings $u$ and $s$ or $LCP(u,s)$ be an index $i$ such that $u_j=s_j$ for each $j\in\{1,\dots,i\}$, and $u_{i+1}\neq s_{i+1}$.
elements.sent-2411-058: For a suffix array $suf$ we can define an array of longest common prefixes (LCP array).
elements.sent-2411-059: Let call it $lcp=(lcp_{1 ,\dots, lcp_{l-1 )$, where $lcp_i=LCP(u[suf_i:l],u[suf_{i+1 :l])$ is the longest common prefix of two suffixes $u[suf_i:l]$ and $u[suf_{i+1 :l]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The array can be constructed with linear complexity:.
elements.sent-2411-061: Using the lcp array, we can compute the LCP of any two suffixes $LCP(u[suf_i:l],u[suf_{j :l])$ in constant time due to the algorithm from \cite{bfc2000 .}}
elements.sent-2411-062: For $i,j\in\{1,\dots |u|\}$, let $LCPSuf(u,i,j)$ be the procedure that returns $LCP(u[suf_i:l],u[suf_{j :l])$, and $PreprocessingForLCP(u)$ be the preprocessing procedure for this algorithm and constructing the $lcp$ array.}
elements.sent-2411-063: One of the most popular computation models for quantum algorithms is the query model.
elements.sent-2411-064: We use the standard form of the quantum query model.
elements.sent-2411-066: Our goal is to compute it on an input $x\in D$.
elements.sent-2411-067: We are given oracle access to the input $x$, i.e. it is implemented by a specific unitary transformation usually defined as $\ket{i}\ket{z}\ket{w}\mapsto \ket{i}\ket{z+x_i\pmod{2 }\ket{w}$, where the $\ket{i}$ register indicates the index of the variable we are querying, $\ket{z}$ is the output register, and $\ket{w}$ is some auxiliary work-space.}
elements.sent-2411-068: An algorithm in the query model consists of alternating applications of arbitrary unitaries which are independent of the input and the query unitary, and a measurement at the end.
elements.sent-2411-069: The smallest number of queries for an algorithm that outputs $f(x)$ with probability $\geq \frac{2 {3}$ on all $x$ is called the quantum query complexity of the function $f$ and is denoted by $Q(f)$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: We refer the readers to \cite{nc2010,a2017,aazksw2019part1,k2022lecturenotes for more details on quantum computing.}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this paper, we are interested in the query complexity of the quantum algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-072: We use modifications of Grover's search algorithm \cite{g96,bbht98 as quantum subroutines.}
elements.sent-2411-073: For these subroutines, time complexity can be obtained from query complexity by multiplication to $O(\log D)$, where $D$ is the size of a search space \cite{ad2017,g2002 .}
elements.section-2411-003: Quantum Algorithm for the Multiple String Matching Problem
elements.sent-2411-074: Let us present a quantum algorithm for the Multiple String Matching Problem.
elements.sent-2411-075: A typical solution for the problem is the Aho–Corasick Algorithm that works with $O(n+L)$ query and time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-076: Here we present an algorithm that works with $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n +m\log n)$ query complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The algorithm uses ideas from the paper of Manber and Myers \cite{mm90 and uses quantum strings comparing algorithm \cite{bjdb2017,ki2019,kiv2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-078: Firstly, let us construct a suffix array, LCP array, and invoke preprocessing for $LCPSuf$ function for the string $t$ (see Section \ref{sec:prelims for details).. Note that $n=|t|$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: Let us consider a string $s^j$ for $j\in\{1,\dots, m\}$.
elements.sent-2411-080: If $s^j$ is a substring of $t$ starting from an index $i$, then $s^j$ is a prefix of the suffix $t[suf_i:n]$, i.e. $t[suf_i:suf_i+|s^j|-1]=s^j$.
elements.sent-2411-081: Since $t[suf_i:n]$ strings are in a lexicographical order according to $suf$ order, all suffixes that have $s^j$ as prefixes are situated sequentially.
elements.sent-2411-082: Our goal is to find two indexes $left_j$ and $right_j$ such that all suffixes $t[suf_i:n]$ has $s^j$ as a prefix for each $i\in\{suf_{left_j ,\dots,suf_{right_j}\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: There is a quantum algorithm for computing LCP of $u$ and $v$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Its complexity is presented in the next lemma.
elements.sent-2411-087: The error probability is at most $0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-088: The algorithm is an application of the First-One Search algorithm \cite{dhhm2006,k2014,ll2016,kkmsy2022 that finds a minimal argument of a Boolean-valued function (predicate) that has the $1$-result.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The First-One Search algorithm algorithm has an error probability $0.1$ that is why we have the same error probability for $QLCP$ procedure.
elements.sent-2411-090: The First-One Search is an algorithm that is based on the Grover Search algorithm \cite{g96,bbht98 .}
elements.sent-2411-091: So, the difference between query and time complexity is a log factor due to \cite{ad2017,g2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-092: The $QLCP(u,v)$ procedure is similar to the quantum algorithm that compares two strings $u$ and $v$ in lexicographical order that was independently developed in \cite{bjdb2017 and in \cite{ki2019,kiv2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-093: For $i\in\{1,\dots,min(|u|,|v|)\}$, let $QLCP(u,v,i)$ be a similar function that computes LCP starting from the $i$-th symbol.. Formally, $QLCP(u,v,i)=QLCP(u[i:|u|],v[i:|v|])$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Due to Lemma \ref{lm:qlcp , complexity of $QLCP(u,v,i)$ is following.}
elements.sent-2411-095: There is a quantum algorithm that implements $QLCP(u,v,i)$ procedure, and has $O(\sqrt{d-i )$ query complexity and $O(\sqrt{d-i}\log (d-i))$ time complexity, where $d$ is the minimal index of an unequal symbol of $u$ and $v$ such that $d>i$.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Let us present the algorithm that finds $left_j$.
elements.sent-2411-098: The algorithm is based on the Binary search algorithm \cite{cormen2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Let $Le$ be the left border of the search segment, and $Ri$ be the right border of the search segment.
elements.sent-2411-102: Let $Llcp\getsQLCP(St_{Le ,s^j)$ be the LCP of the first suffix and the searching string.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Let $Rlcp\getsQLCP(St_{Ri ,s^j)$ be the LCP of the last suffix and the searching string.}
elements.sent-2411-104: If $Llcp<|s^j|$ and $s^j<St_1$, i.e. $Llcp<|s^j|$ and $s^j[Llcp+1]<St_1[Llcp+1]$, then we can say that $s^j$ is less than any suffix of $t$ and is not a prefix of any suffix of $t$.
elements.sent-2411-105: So, we can say that $I_j$ is empty.
elements.sent-2411-106: In that case, we stop the algorithm, otherwise, we continue with Step 3.
elements.sent-2411-107: If $Rlcp<|s^j|$ and $s^j>St_n$, i.e. $Rlcp<|s^j|$ and $s^j[Rlcp+1]>St_n[Rlcp+1]$, then we can say that $s^j$ greats any suffix of $t$ and is not a prefix of any suffix of $t$.
elements.sent-2411-108: So, we can say that $I_j$ is empty also.
elements.sent-2411-109: In that case, we stop the algorithm, otherwise, we continue with Step 4.
elements.sent-2411-110: We repeat the next steps while $Ri-Le>1$, otherwise we go to Step 9.
elements.sent-2411-112: If $Llcp\geq Rlcp$, then we go to Step 7, and to Step 8 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-114: Note that $LCP(St_{Le ,St_M)$ can be computed as $LCPSuf(t,Le,M)$ in $O(1)$ query and time complexity.}

elements.sent-2411-117: So, we can assign $Le\gets M$ and not change $Llcp$.
elements.sent-2411-118: If $LCP(St_{Le ,St_M)=Llcp$, then all suffixes from $St_{Le}$ to $St_M$ has at least $Llcp$ common symbols with $s^j$.}

elements.sent-2411-136: The implementation for $LeftBorderSearch(j)$ is presented in Algorithm~\ref{alg:leftSearch , and the complexity is presented in Lemma \ref{lm:leftSearch .}}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Quantum algorithm for $LeftBorderSearch(j)$ that searches $left_j$.}\label{alg:leftSearch} \begin{algorithmic} \State $Llcp \gets QLCP(St_{1}, s^j)$, $Rlcp \gets QLCP(St_{n}, s^j)$ \If{$Llcp = |s^j|$} \State $answer \gets 0$ \Elif{$s^j[Llcp+1]<St_1[Llcp+1]$\Then} \State $answer \gets -1$ \Elif{$s^j[Rlcp+1]>St_n[Rlcp+1]$\Then} \State $answer \gets -1$ \Else \State $Le\gets 1$, $Ri\gets n$ \While{$Ri - Le > 1$} \State $M \gets \lfloor (Le + Ri) / 2\rfloor$ \If{$Llcp\geq Rlcp$} \If{$LCPSuf(Le, M)> Llcp$} \State $Le\gets M$ \EndIf \If{$LCPSuf(Le, M)= Llcp$} \State $Mlcp=QLCP(St_M, s^j, Llcp+1)$ \If{$Mlcp=|s^j| \Or St_M[Mlcp+1]>s^j[Mlcp+1]$} \State $Ri\gets M$, $Rlcp\gets Mlcp$ \Else \State $Le\gets M$, $Llcp\gets Mlcp$ \EndIf \EndIf \If{$LCPSuf(Le, M)< Llcp$} \State $Ri \gets M$, $Rlcp \gets LCPSuf(Le, M)$ \EndIf \Else \If{$LCPSuf(M, Ri)> Rlcp$} \State $Ri\gets M$ \EndIf \If{$LCPSuf(M, Ri)= Rlcp$} \State $Mlcp=QLCP(St_M, s^j, Rlcp+1)$ \If{$Mlcp=|s^j| \Or St_M[Mlcp+1]>s^j[Mlcp+1]$} \State $Ri\gets M$, $Rlcp\gets Mlcp$ \Else \State $Le\gets M$, $Llcp\gets Mlcp$ \EndIf \EndIf \If{$LCPSuf(M, Ri)< Rlcp$} \State $Le \gets M$, $Llcp \gets LCPSuf(M, Ri)$ \EndIf \EndIf \EndWhile \State $answer \gets Ri$ \EndIf \State \Return $answer$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-137: Algorithm \ref{alg:leftSearch implements $LeftBorderSearch(j)$, searches $left_j$ and works with $O(\sqrt{|s^j|\log n +\log n)$ query complexity, $O(\sqrt{|s^j|\log n}\log|s^j|+\log n)$ time complexity, and at most $0.1$ error probability.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: It is always increased and never decreased.
elements.sent-2411-140: Assume that during the algorithm, it has values $Llcp_1,\dots, Llcp_d$.
elements.sent-2411-141: We can say that $Llcp_1<\dots< Llcp_d$, and $d\leq \log_2 n$ because Binary search do at most $\log_2 n$ steps.
elements.sent-2411-144: Query complexity of changing $Llcp$ from $Llcp_i$ to $Llcp_{i+1}$ is $O(\sqrt{Llcp_{i+1 -Llcp_{i )$ due to Corollary \ref{cr:lcp .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: So, the total query complexity is \begin{equation}\sqrt{Llcp_{1}} + \sum\limits_{i=1}^{d-1}O(\sqrt{Llcp_{i+1}-Llcp_{i}})=O\left(\sqrt{d(Llcp_{1} + \sum\limits_{i=1}^{d-1}(Llcp_{i+1}-Llcp_{i}))}\right)\end{equation} due to Cauchy--Bunyakovsky--Schwarz inequality.
elements.sent-2411-148: Finally, the complexity of all changing of $Llcp$ is $O(\sqrt{d|s^j| )=O(\sqrt{|s^j|\log n )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Using the same technique, we can show that the time complexity is $O(\sqrt{|s^j|\log n}\log|s^j|)$ Similarly, we can see that the complexity of all changing of $Rlcp$ is $O(\sqrt{d|s^j| )=O(\sqrt{|s^j|\log n )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: All other parts of the step of Binary search work with $O(1)$ query and time complexity, including $LCPSuf$ due to Lemma~\ref{lm:lcp2-arr .}
elements.sent-2411-151: They give us additional $O(\log n)$ complexity.
elements.sent-2411-152: Note that each invocation of $QLCP$ has an error probability $0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-153: Therefore, $O(\log n)$ invocations of the procedure have an error probability that is close to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-154: At the same time, the whole Algorithm \ref{alg:leftSearch is a sequence of First-One Search procedures such that next invocation of the First-One Search uses results of the previous invocations.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Due to \cite{k2014 , such sequence can be converted to an algorithm with the same total complexity and error probability $0.1$.. \Endproof.}
elements.sent-2411-156: The whole algorithm is presented in Algorithm \ref{alg:search , and complexity is discussed in Theorem \ref{th:allSearch.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Quantum algorithm for the Multiple String Matching Problem.}\label{alg:search} \begin{algorithmic} \State $ConstructSuffixArray(t)$ \State $PreprocessingForLCP(t)$ \For{$j\in\{1,\dots,m\}$} \State $left_j\gets LeftBorderSearch(j)$ \State $right_j\gets RightBorderSearch(j)$ \If{$left_j=-1\Or right_j=-1$} \State $I_j\gets ()$ \Else \State $I_j\gets (suf_{left_j},\dots,suf_{right_j})$ \EndIf \EndFor \State \Return $(I_1,\dots,I_m)$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-157: Algorithm \ref{alg:search solves the Multiple String Matching Problem and works with $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n +m\log n)$ query complexity, $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n}\log b+m\log n)$ time complexity and at most $0.1$ error probability.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: Firstly, complexity of $ConstructSuffixArray(t)$ and $PreprocessingForLCP(t)$ is $O(n)$ due to Lemmas \ref{lm:suf-arr , \ref{lm:lcp-arr , and \ref{lm:lcp2-arr .}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Query complexity of $LeftBorderSearch(j)$ and $RightBorderSearch(j)$ is $O(\sqrt{|s^j|\log n +\log n)$, and time complexity is $O(\sqrt{|s^j|\log n}\log |s^j|+\log n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-160: So, the total query complexity of these procedures for all $j\in\{1,\dots,m\}$ is \begin{equation}O\left(m\log n +\sum_{j=1}^m\sqrt{|s^j|\log n}\right)=O\left(m\log n +\sqrt{\log n}\sum_{j=1}^m\sqrt{|s^j|}\right)=\end{equation}\begin{equation}O\left(m\log n +\sqrt{\log n\cdot m\sum_{j=1}^m|s^j|}\right)=O\left(m\log n +\sqrt{mL\log n}\right)\end{equation} We get such a result because of Cauchy--Bunyakovsky--Schwarz inequality.
elements.sent-2411-161: Similarly, time complexity is $O\left(m\log n +\sqrt{mL\log n}\log b\right)$ since $b=max\{|s^1|,\dots,|s^m|\}$.
elements.sent-2411-162: The total query complexity is $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n +m\log n)$, and time complexity is $O(n+\sqrt{mL\log n}\log d+m\log b)$.}
elements.sent-2411-163: Note that, each invocation of $LeftBorderSearch(j)$ and $RightBorderSearch(j)$ has error probability $0.1$.
elements.sent-2411-164: Therefore, $O(m)$ invocations of the procedure have an error probability that is close to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-165: At the same time, the algorithm is a sequence of First-One Search procedures.
elements.sent-2411-166: Let us present the lower bound for the problem.
elements.sent-2411-167: The lower bound for query complexity of the Multiple String Matching Problem is $\Omega(n+L)$ in the classical case, and $\Omega(n+\sqrt{mL )$ in the quantum case.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Let us consider a binary alphabet for simplicity.. Let $s^1=``1''$.
elements.sent-2411-169: Then, the complexity of searching positions of $s^1$ in $t$ is at least as hard as an unstructured search of all positions of $1$ among $n$ bits.
elements.sent-2411-170: Due to \cite{bbbv1997 , classical query complexity of this problem is $\Omega(n)$ and quantum query complexity is $\Omega(\sqrt{nt )$, where $t$ is the number of occurrences $1$ among $n$ bits.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: In the worst case, it is also $\Omega(n)$.
elements.sent-2411-172: Let us consider $t$ as a string of $0$s.
elements.sent-2411-173: In that case, if we have any symbol $1$ in a dictionary string, then this string does not occur in $t$.
elements.sent-2411-174: We should find all such strings for forming results for these strings.

elements.sent-2411-178: Due to \cite{bbbv1997 , classical query complexity of this problem is $\Omega(n)$ and quantum query complexity is $\Omega(\sqrt{mL )$ So, the total lower bound is $\Omega(max(n,L))=\Omega(n+L)$ in the classical case and $\Omega(max(\sqrt{n ,\sqrt{mL ))=\Omega(\sqrt{n +\sqrt{mL )$ in the quantum case.. \Endproof.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-179: In the paper, we present a quantum algorithm for the Multiple String Matching Problem that works with $O(n+\sqrt{mL +m\log n)$ query complexity and error probability $0.1$.}
elements.sent-2411-180: It is better than the classical counterparts if $O(m)$ strings of the dictionary have at least $\omega(\log n)$ length.
